full_response:
Below is a concise, step-by-step derivation of the definition.

1.  Choice of metric and signature  
    We work in Minkowski spacetime with coordinates \(x^\mu=(ct,x,y,z)\) and metric  
    \[
      g_{\mu\nu} = \mathrm{diag}(-1,\,+1,\,+1,\,+1)\,.
    \]

2.  Definition of the Minkowski “norm” (inner product) of a 4-vector  
    For any 4-vector \(V^\mu\), its squared norm is
    \[
      V^2 \;\equiv\; g_{\mu\nu}\,V^\mu V^\nu
      \;=\;-\,\bigl(V^0\bigr)^2 + \bigl(V^1\bigr)^2 + \bigl(V^2\bigr)^2 + \bigl(V^3\bigr)^2
      \,,
    \]
    where \(V^0=ct\)-component and \(V^{1,2,3}\) are spatial components.

3.  Classification of 4-vectors by their norm  
    • Timelike if \(V^2<0\)  
    • Spacelike if \(V^2>0\)  
    • Null (lightlike) if \(V^2=0\,\).

4.  Definition of a null vector  
    A 4-vector \(k^\mu\) is called null (or lightlike) precisely when
    \[
      g_{\mu\nu}\,k^\mu k^\nu \;=\;0\,.
    \]
    Equivalently, its Minkowski “length” vanishes.

5.  Geometric/physical interpretation  
    Null vectors are tangent to the worldlines of massless particles (e.g.\ photons), and they span the light cone at each event.

Final concise statement:

\boxed{\text{A null (lightlike) vector }k^\mu\text{ is one whose Minkowski norm vanishes, }g_{\mu\nu}k^\mu k^\nu=0\,.}

Confidence: 100%
extracted_answer: \text{A null (lightlike) vector }k^\mu\text{ is one whose Minkowski norm vanishes, }g_{\mu\nu}k^\mu k^\nu=0\,.
